content.text: A CSS methodology is a set of conventions — naming rules, file structure, and authoring principles — that keeps stylesheets readable, scalable, and maintainable as a codebase grows. Without one, CSS tends toward cascading overrides, ever-rising specificity wars, and styles that nobody dares delete.

Angular's component model already provides style isolation through ViewEncapsulation, but choosing a methodology within each component's SCSS file still matters: it keeps class names predictable, prevents internal specificity fights, and makes the component's structure obvious from its template.

attrs.alt: CSS methodologies overview: BEM, ITCSS, OOCSS, and Utility-First

content.text: BEM is the most widely used CSS naming convention. Every class belongs to exactly one of three categories:
— a standalone, self-contained component. Names are lowercase with optional hyphens:
— a part of the block that has no meaning outside it. Joined to the block with two underscores:
— a flag that changes appearance or behaviour. Joined to block or element with two hyphens:

Article headline

Summary text...
Never represent the DOM hierarchy with nested BEM names: .card__body__title is wrong. Use .card__title inside .card__body.
Elements belong to blocks, not to other elements. Go as flat as possible.

content: A block can contain another block:

content.text: Modifiers never stand alone — always apply the base class too: class="btn btn--primary", not just class="btn--primary".
Angular component host element acts as the block root. Use :host {} for block-level styles.
Each Angular component naturally maps to one BEM block. The component selector name is the block name.
Nicole Sullivan's OOCSS (2009) introduced two core principles that BEM and most later methodologies absorbed:

A reusable structural object (.btn) carries only layout properties — padding, border-radius, display. A skin class (.btn-primary) carries only visual properties — background, color, border-color. Combine them freely.
An object should look the same regardless of where it appears. Avoid rules like .sidebar .heading { font-size: 0.9rem } because they tie .heading's appearance to its container.

Harry Roberts' ITCSS organises an entire project's stylesheets into layers of decreasing reach and increasing specificity, preventing specificity wars by ensuring styles are always written before they are overridden.
SCSS variables, CSS custom property declarations, breakpoint maps
SCSS mixins and functions — respond-to(), rem(), z()
Normalize, reset, box-sizing. Selectors: *, html, body
Bare HTML element defaults — a, p, h1–h6, img
BEM UI components — .card, .btn, .nav. Bulk of your CSS
In Angular, ITCSS layers 1–4 live in your global styles.scss, layers 5–6 live inside component SCSS files, and layer 7 lives in a global utilities partial imported last.
Popularised by Tailwind CSS, utility-first forgoes semantic class names entirely. Every class does one thing: .flex, .items-center, .p-4, .text-xl. You compose visual designs directly in HTML.

Alice

Senior Engineer
no naming decisions, no CSS bloat (Tailwind's PurgeCSS removes unused utilities), no context-switching between HTML and CSS, faster iteration.
HTML becomes verbose, class strings are hard to scan, semantic meaning disappears from markup, refactoring means updating every template.

npm install -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init

Angular ViewEncapsulation does not interfere with Tailwind — utility classes are global and that is intentional. Avoid mixing component-scoped SCSS BEM styles and Tailwind in the same component; pick one approach per component, ideally per project.
Separate structure from skin; container independence
Layer stylesheets by specificity and reach
Works — but conflicts with encapsulation philosophy
Locally-scoped class names via build tooling
Real Angular projects often combine approaches:
— global file organisation prevents specificity wars.
— predictable naming within each component's SCSS.
— .sr-only, .u-truncate cover edge cases without hacking component styles.

src/
  styles/
    settings/  _tokens.scss   _breakpoints.scss
    tools/     _mixins.scss   _functions.scss
    generic/   _reset.scss    _normalize.scss
    elements/  _base.scss
    objects/   _container.scss  _grid.scss  _btn.scss
    utilities/ _helpers.scss
  styles.scss   ← @use all layers in order
  app/
    components/
      card/card.component.scss   ← BEM within the component
Add stylelint to enforce your chosen methodology automatically:
in CI to catch naming violations before they merge. BEM violations are especially easy to miss during rapid feature work.
Keep elements one level below the block; use a new block if nesting grows beyond two levels.
A .btn-primary that adds padding is breaking the separation; padding belongs in .btn.
Using BEM in one component and utility-first in another breeds confusion. Agree on one approach per project layer.
Loading utilities before components means overrides don't work. Layer order is non-negotiable.
If you break out of ViewEncapsulation with ::ng-deep to apply BEM modifiers, you undermine the isolation model. Prefer @Input-driven host class bindings instead.
Deep Dive: BEM Anti-Patterns and Refactoring
BEM is easy to misapply. Here are the most common patterns you will encounter in existing codebases and how to fix them:
Anti-Pattern 3: State Classes Without BEM Convention
list all class names in a component. Group them into logical blocks.
each self-contained UI component gets a block name.
rename class attributes in HTML; use ngClass binding if dynamic.
SMACSS — Scalable and Modular Architecture for CSS
SMACSS (Jonathan Snook, 2011) divides CSS into five categories. It influenced Angular's own style guide and is worth understanding even if you do not adopt it wholesale.
Prefix with l-. Define page structure.
Prefix with is-. Often toggled by JS/Angular.
Prefix with t-. Override module appearance.
In Angular apps, SMACSS Layout classes belong in the global stylesheet. SMACSS Modules map to components. SMACSS State maps to BEM modifiers or Angular host class bindings.
CSS-in-JS and CSS Modules (and Why Angular Doesn't Need Them)
React's ecosystem spawned two alternative approaches to style scoping that are worth understanding:
: build-tool transforms class names to unique hashes.
at compile time. Solves global leakage but requires import in JS.
(styled-components, Emotion): styles are strings or tagged literals inside TS/JS. Enables dynamic styling with props. Runtime overhead and no syntax highlighting are the trade-offs.

Angular's ViewEncapsulation provides the same guarantee as CSS Modules without the build complexity: styles defined in a component's styleUrls are automatically scoped to that component's DOM via attribute selectors (_ngcontent-xyz). You get isolation without renaming classes or importing style objects.

The only case where CSS-in-JS adds something Angular's model doesn't is truly dynamic theming (styles computed from runtime data). In Angular, CSS custom properties (var(--x)) cover that use case better — they cross ViewEncapsulation and update without re-rendering.
Choosing and Enforcing a Methodology in Your Team
larger teams benefit more from strict naming conventions (BEM + ITCSS).
OOCSS objects + BEM components are the standard foundation.
Gradually introduce BEM/ITCSS — do not try to rename everything at once.
ViewEncapsulation already solves global leakage; focus methodology effort on naming (BEM) and global file organisation (ITCSS).
. Fail the build on naming violations early — before a PR is reviewed.
Document your project's methodology choice in a CONTRIBUTING.md section called "CSS Conventions". New team members will apply it correctly if the rules are written down and enforced automatically.

CSS methodologies impose structure on an inherently loose language. BEM provides predictable naming at the component level and maps naturally to Angular's component model. ITCSS organises the global stylesheet to prevent specificity conflicts across a large project. OOCSS separates structure from skin, enabling composable objects. Utility-first trades semantics for speed and works in Angular but conflicts with the encapsulation philosophy.

In practice, combine them: ITCSS at the project level, BEM within components, OOCSS for shared objects, utilities for exceptional cases. Enforce your conventions with stylelint from the start — retrofitting naming rules onto a large codebase is costly.
